quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 7, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Nomenclature Practice: Hydrates, Acids, and Mixed Compounds
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Naming Ionic Compounds, Hydrates, Acids, Transition Metals, Polyatomic Ions, Formula Writing

questions.question: The formula for magnesium sulfate is:
questions.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is MgSO₄. Magnesium (Mg²⁺) combines with sulfate (SO₄²⁻) in a 1:1 ratio. MgS (A) is magnesium sulfide, MgSO₃ (D) is magnesium sulfite.

questions.question: The formula for sodium sulfide is:
questions.explanation: Sodium sulfide is Na₂S. Sodium (Na⁺) has a +1 charge, and sulfide (S²⁻) has a -2 charge, so two sodium ions are needed. NaS (A) would be charge-imbalanced.

questions.question: The name for NH₄NO₃ is:
questions.explanation: NH₄NO₃ is named ammonium nitrate. It contains the ammonium ion (NH₄⁺) and the nitrate ion (NO₃⁻).

questions.question: The name for Ba(OH)₂ is:
questions.explanation: Ba(OH)₂ is named barium hydroxide. It contains the hydroxide ion (OH⁻). Barium (Group 2) forms only one type of ion (+2), so no Roman numeral is needed.

questions.question: The name for KHCO₃ is:
questions.explanation: KHCO₃ is named potassium hydrogen carbonate (or potassium bicarbonate). It contains the hydrogen carbonate ion (HCO₃⁻).

questions.question: The name for CuSO₄·5H₂O is:
questions.explanation: CuSO₄·5H₂O is named copper(II) sulfate pentahydrate. Copper has a +2 charge (indicated by II), and 'pentahydrate' indicates five water molecules. Option B omits the necessary Roman numeral.

questions.question: Give the formula for cobalt(II) chlorate dihydrate:
questions.explanation: Cobalt(II) chlorate dihydrate is Co(ClO₃)₂·2H₂O. Cobalt(II) is Co²⁺, chlorate is ClO₃⁻, so two chlorates are needed. The dot notation indicates two water molecules of hydration.

questions.question: Name the compound Co(NO₃)₂:
questions.explanation: Co(NO₃)₂ is named cobalt(II) nitrate. Nitrate (NO₃⁻) has a -1 charge, so two nitrates give -2, meaning cobalt has a +2 charge, indicated by (II).

questions.question: Which is the formula for lead(IV) chloride?
questions.explanation: Lead(IV) chloride is PbCl₄. Lead(IV) has a +4 charge, and chloride (Cl⁻) has a -1 charge, so four chlorides are needed. PbCl₂ (A) would be lead(II) chloride.

questions.question: Name the compound CrO₃:
questions.explanation: CrO₃ is named chromium(VI) oxide. Oxygen has a -2 charge, so three oxygens give -6. Therefore, chromium must have a +6 charge, indicated by (VI). It is also commonly called chromium trioxide, but the systematic Stock system name with Roman numeral is chromium(VI) oxide.

questions.question: Name the compound N₂O₄:
questions.explanation: N₂O₄ is named dinitrogen tetroxide. It is a covalent compound, so prefixes are used: 'di-' for two nitrogen atoms and 'tetra-' for four oxygen atoms. Nitrous oxide (A) is N₂O, dinitrogen pentoxide (B) is N₂O₅, and nitrogen oxide (C) is too vague.

questions.question: Name the acid H₃PO₄ (dissolved in water):
questions.explanation: H₃PO₄ is named phosphoric acid. It contains the phosphate ion (PO₄³⁻). Phosphorous acid (B) would be H₃PO₃ (phosphite).

questions.question: Name the acid H₂SO₃ (dissolved in water):
questions.explanation: H₂SO₃ is named sulfurous acid. It contains the sulfite ion (SO₃²⁻). Sulfuric acid (A) would be H₂SO₄ (sulfate).

questions.question: The chemical formula for iron(II) nitrate is:
questions.explanation: Iron(II) nitrate is Fe(NO₃)₂. Iron(II) has a +2 charge (Fe²⁺), and nitrate is NO₃⁻ with a -1 charge, so two nitrates are needed. Fe(NO₂)₂ (B) would be iron(II) nitrite.

questions.question: Name the compound Co₂(SO₃)₃:
questions.explanation: Co₂(SO₃)₃ is named cobalt(III) sulfite. Sulfite (SO₃²⁻) has a -2 charge, so three sulfites give -6. Therefore, two cobalts total +6, meaning each cobalt has a +3 charge, indicated by (III).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered naming hydrates, acids, and complex ionic compounds! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the difference between -ate and -ite endings and hydrate notation.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on determining oxidation states for transition metals and naming acids.
scoreMessages.0: Please review naming rules for ionic compounds with transition metals, acids, and hydrates.
